perturbation.original_text: 4.7 Data protection. Each Party agrees to comply with all applicable data protection and privacy laws arising from its obligations, if any, under the Agreement and to reasonably co-operate with the other Party in order to allow the other Party to comply with any laws as deemed necessary from time to time. Each Party shall treat all data, information relating to Services, including but not limit to the customer personal data, as confidential. Each party warrants that it shall take appropriate technical and organizational measures against unauthorized or unlawful processing of personal data and against accidental loss or destruction of, or damage to, the personal data of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.7 Data protection. Each Party agrees to comply with data protection and privacy laws as they deem necessary. Each Party shall treat data relating to Services as confidential. Each party warrants that it shall take measures against loss or damage to the personal data of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires compliance with 'all applicable' data protection laws and specifies 'appropriate technical and organizational measures.' The modified version removes 'all applicable', significantly weakening the commitment and allows each party to choose compliance based on what 'they deem necessary'. It also removes 'unauthorized or unlawful processing' as a risk factor, and removes 'technical and organizational' from the types of measures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Information Technology (Reasonable security practices and procedures and sensitive personal data or information) Rules, 2011
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 4 and Rule 8 of Information Technology (Reasonable security practices and procedures and sensitive personal data or information) Rules, 2011
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 4 mandates the implementation of reasonable security practices and procedures for sensitive personal data or information. Rule 8 mandates implementing a comprehensive security policy to protect sensitive personal data. The modified text removes the requirement to comply with 'all applicable' laws, and weakens the security measures, thus contradicting the comprehensive data protection standards required under Indian law. It also takes away the aspect of preventing unauthorized or unlawful processing. It fails to ensure reasonable security practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Elevating the duty to ensure psychosocial safety: New law and key compliance considerations (Part 1) - Health & Safety - Australia ARTICLE 13 April 2023 Elevating the duty to ensure psychosocial safety: New law and key compliance considerations (Part 1) HR Holding Redlich More Contributor Holding Redlich, a national commercial law firm with offices in Melbourne, Canberra, Sydney, Brisbane, and Cairns, delivers tailored solutions with expert legal thinking and industry knowledge, prioritizing client partnerships. Explore Firm Details Part 1 provides an overview of the legislation and key considerations regarding psychosocial hazards in the workplace. Australia Employment and HR Rachel Drew and Keisha Currie Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. Safe Work Australia developed model Work Health and Safety laws ( model WHS laws ) in 2011, which most states (except Victoria) have now implemented with minor variations. The laws comprise of the model WHS Act, model WHS Regulations and model Codes of Practice. With managing psychosocial hazards in the workplace being an important responsibility for all employers, especially human resources managers, it is essential for businesses to understand Australia's existing health and safety law framework and key considerations when addressing these hazards at work . In this three-part series, we look at various codes of practice in relation to psychosocial hazards in the workplace and how businesses can implement these into their operations. Our first instalment provides an overview of the relevant legislation and key considerations. Refresher on Australia's health and safety law framework A review of the model WHS laws conducted between 2017 and 2018 (the Boland Review) identified that psychological health was neglected in the model WHS Regulations and the model Codes of Practice. Recommendations included additional regulations to be developed regarding methods in identifying psychosocial risks in the workplace and the appropriate control measures to manage those risks. In July 2022, Safe Work Australia updated the model WHS Regulations to incorporate those recommendations in relation to psychosocial risks in the workplace. At the same time, Safe Work Australia also published the final version of a new model "Code of Practice: Managing psychosocial hazards at work", providing practical guidance on how to identify, manage and control psychosocial hazards in the workplace. Each Australian state has taken a different approach to the model Regulations and Code, requiring human resources managers to understand and apply different rules in each state a business operates in. A code of practice The model WHS Act defines "approved code of practice" as a code of practice approved under Part 14 of the model WHS Act. The relevant sections under Part 14 are sections 274 an
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens data protection obligations. By removing 'all applicable' data protection laws and allowing each party to comply with data protection and privacy laws 'as they deem necessary,' the modified clause creates a loophole where parties can selectively comply, potentially leading to non-compliance. The change also eliminates the requirement for 'appropriate technical and organizational measures' against 'unauthorized or unlawful processing,' and only focuses on 'loss or damage'. The snippet on psychosocial safety in Australia highlights the importance of understanding and applying different rules, suggesting that adherence to all applicable laws is essential, which is undermined by the modified clause's selective compliance approach. The removal of specifics weakens the legally binding nature of the clause.

perturbation.original_text: 4.8 Both Parties acknowledge and confirm that the Reserve Bank of India (hereafter "RBI") or persons authorized by it has right to access and inspect the PC Financial's documents, records of Service transactions and other necessary information related to the Services or Activity given to, stored or processed by Mobimagic within a reasonable time.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.8 Both Parties acknowledge that the Reserve Bank of India (hereafter "RBI") may request access to PC Financial's documents related to the Services.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause acknowledges RBI's *right* to access and inspect documents, records, and information. The modified version changes this to a mere possibility that RBI 'may request' access. It also removes the explicit mention of 'records of Service transactions and other necessary information', and the phrase 'within a reasonable time', thus limiting the scope and timeframe of potential RBI oversight, and creating ambiguity around the information subject to access.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 45Q of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934
perturbation.law_citation: Section 45Q of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 45Q of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934, explicitly states that the provisions of the Act shall override other laws. By weakening the acknowledgement of RBI's right to access information, the modified clause undermines the statutory powers granted to the RBI under this Act to regulate and supervise financial institutions, including NBFCs like PC Financial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: AdvocateKhoj - 404 Page 404 UH OH! You're lost. The page you are looking for does not exist. How you got here is a mystery. But you can click the button below to go back to the homepage. HOME
perturbation.justification: The original contract acknowledges RBI's right to access and inspect documents, while the modified version weakens this to a mere possibility that RBI 'may request' access. The Perturbation Explanation points out that the change limits the scope and timeframe of potential RBI oversight. The Law Explanation highlights that Section 45Q of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934, explicitly states that the provisions of the Act shall override other laws, granting RBI statutory powers to regulate and supervise financial institutions. This weakening undermines the statutory powers granted to the RBI. The scraped law snippets provide no direct evidence to support or refute this claim; however, the absence of the snippets does not invalidate the argument presented. The omission of the explicit right of access and the change to a mere request creates a contradiction by undermining the regulatory authority of the RBI.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. PC Financial shall pay to Mobimagic a fixed service fee in the amount of 970,000 United States Dollars (USD 970,000) for each month (the "Fee") within the timeline as permitted under the prevailing India regulations. It is stipulated in this Agreement that Mobimagic will send an invoice to PC Financial on monthly basis according to the progress of the service. After PC Financial confirms that invoice contents are correct, PC Financial shall pay within 45 days after confirming thus.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. PC Financial shall pay to Mobimagic a fixed service fee in the amount of 970,000 United States Dollars (USD 970,000) for each month (the "Fee"). It is stipulated in this Agreement that Mobimagic will send an invoice to PC Financial on monthly basis according to the progress of the service. PC Financial shall pay within 90 days of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires payments to be made 'within the timeline as permitted under the prevailing India regulations' and 'within 45 days after confirming' the invoice. The modification removes the reference to Indian regulations and extends the payment timeframe to a flat 90 days 'of invoice', regardless of any regulatory stipulations or confirmation of the invoice, potentially violating regulations regarding timely payments to vendors and service providers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 43B of the Income Tax Act, 1961
perturbation.law_citation: Section 43B of the Income Tax Act, 1961
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 43B of the Income Tax Act, 1961 disallows certain deductions if they are not paid within the time prescribed under the respective laws. While this applies primarily to statutory dues, delayed payments can create tax implications and demonstrate financial instability and failure to adhere to prevailing Indian regulatory norms. The new text of the contract allows payments in 90 days, removing the link to 'prevailing Indian regulations,' possibly contradicting the conditions established in 43B.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates adherence to prevailing Indian regulations for payment timelines, while the changed text extends the payment period to a fixed 90 days, omitting this crucial regulatory compliance clause. This omission potentially contradicts Section 43B of the Income Tax Act, 1961, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which addresses the disallowance of certain deductions if payments are not made within the prescribed time under the relevant laws. The altered text makes the contract non-compliant by removing the direct connection to these regulations, creating a legal contradiction because it removes essential information pertaining to legally mandated payment standards.
